guards.name: Closed Guard 🔒
guards.description: The closed guard is one of the fundamental positions in Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu. The bottom player wraps their legs around the opponent’s waist and locks their ankles behind the opponent’s back while controlling posture and distance to create sweeps or submissions.
execution: Lie on your back with your opponent between your legs
Lock your ankles behind their back around the lower back or hips
Break your opponent’s posture by pulling them forward with your legs and grips
Control sleeves, collar, wrists, or overhooks to limit their arm movement
Keep your hips active and ready to pivot for angle changes
Squeeze your knees inward to maintain tight control
Use your legs to constantly manage distance and posture
Create attacking angles by shifting your hips side to side
Control one arm or posture before initiating attacks
pros: Excellent control over opponent's posture and distance
Multiple submission opportunities from a secure position
Prevents opponent from advancing position easily
Great for beginners to learn guard fundamentals
Forces the opponent to work hard to open the guard
Safe position to attack from with relatively low risk
Works well for both sport BJJ and self-defense
cons: Can be difficult against opponents who stand to open the guard
Can require significant energy against strong opponents
Requires different control strategies in No-Gi due to fewer grip options
Limited mobility compared to open guard systems
Can be stalled by very defensive opponents
Vulnerable to strong guard breaking strategies
Less dynamic for modern competitive guard systems
tips: Always prioritize breaking your opponent’s posture
Use angle changes to create submission opportunities
Do not allow your opponent to establish strong posture
Use hip movement to adjust guard and create attacks
Open your guard strategically to transition or attack
Chain submissions together when your opponent defends
Use overhooks and underhooks to control the arms
Climb your guard higher on their back when possible
Attack when your opponent pushes into you
Constantly disrupt their balance and posture

sweeps.name: Scissor Sweep
sweeps.description: Classic sweep using a scissoring motion with your legs to off-balance and sweep your opponent.

sweeps.name: Flower Sweep (Pendulum Sweep)
sweeps.description: Use a pendulum motion with your leg while controlling an arm to sweep the opponent.

sweeps.name: Hip Bump Sweep
sweeps.description: Sit up quickly and bump with your hips while controlling an arm to sweep the opponent backwards.

sweeps.name: Overhead Sweep
sweeps.description: Pull your opponent forward and elevate them over your head to reverse the position.

sweeps.name: Kimura Sweep
sweeps.description: Use a kimura grip to break posture and sweep when the opponent defends.

sweeps.name: Sit-Up Sweep
sweeps.description: Sit up into your opponent and drive forward to off-balance and sweep them.

sweeps.name: Clamp Sweep
sweeps.description: Control the opponent’s arm and clamp their shoulder while using your legs to rotate them over.

sweeps.name: Lumberjack Sweep
sweeps.description: When opponent stands, control both ankles and push while pulling to sweep them backwards.

sweeps.name: Arm Drag to Back Take
sweeps.description: Use an arm drag from closed guard to pull the opponent forward and around your body, exposing their back and allowing you to secure back control.

sweeps.name: Muscle Sweep
sweeps.description: A powerful sweep from closed guard that uses upper body strength and timing to elevate and reverse the opponent when they resist posture breaking.

passes.name: Standing Guard Break
passes.description: Stand up while maintaining posture to force the guard open.

passes.name: Knee-in-Tailbone Guard Break
passes.description: Place your knee in the opponent’s tailbone to create pressure and open the guard.

passes.name: Log Splitter Guard Break
passes.description: Control both lapels or torso and drive your knee between the opponent’s legs to break the guard.

passes.name: Sao Paulo Pass
passes.description: Apply strong shoulder pressure while controlling the legs to pass after opening guard.

passes.name: Carlson Gracie Jr. Sitting Guard Break
passes.description: Sit back with strong posture while controlling the lapels or biceps, widen your knees for base, and apply steady outward pressure with your elbows and hips to force the guard open.

passes.name: Standard Standing Closed Guard Opening
passes.description: Control the opponent’s sleeve and lapels, stand with strong posture, step one leg back, and use downward pressure on the hips or thigh to pry the guard open.

passes.name: Handcuff Pass
passes.description: Catch your opponent’s arm in a handcuff position, stand and open the guard keeping the handcuff control.

submissions.name: Triangle Choke
submissions.description: Use your legs to trap the opponent’s neck and arm to create a choke.

submissions.name: Armbar
submissions.description: Isolate the opponent’s arm and extend it over your hips to apply a joint lock.

submissions.name: Cross Collar Choke
submissions.description: Use the opponent’s collar grips to apply a powerful choke.

submissions.name: Guillotine Choke
submissions.description: Wrap your arm around the opponent’s neck and finish while controlling them with closed guard.

submissions.name: Kimura
submissions.description: Attack the opponent’s shoulder with a figure-four grip on the arm.

submissions.name: Omoplata
submissions.description: Use your legs to isolate and attack the opponent’s shoulder joint.

submissions.name: Loop Choke
submissions.description: Feed the collar behind the opponent’s neck to create a tight choking angle.

submissions.name: Gogoplata
submissions.description: Use your shin across the opponent’s throat while controlling their posture.

submissions.name: Baratoplata
submissions.description: Advanced shoulder lock that combines armbar and omoplata mechanics.

submissions.name: Wrist Locks
submissions.description: Attack the wrist when the opponent posts or defends submissions.

guards.name: Spider Guard 🕷️
guards.description: Spider guard is a Gi-based open guard where the bottom player controls the opponent’s sleeves while placing their feet on the opponent’s biceps. By extending and retracting the legs, the guard player controls distance, posture, and balance while creating sweeps and submission opportunities.
execution: Start from open guard with your hips mobile and feet ready to engage
Secure strong grips on both of your opponent’s sleeves
Place your feet on their biceps with your toes pointing outward
Extend your legs to create tension and keep your opponent at distance
Keep your hips slightly off the mat so you can pivot and angle
Alternate between single spider and double spider depending on balance
Use one leg to push while the other pulls to off-balance your opponent
Constantly adjust angles with your hips to create attacks
Be ready to transition to lasso, collar sleeve, De La Riva, or other open guards
pros: Excellent distance control and posture management
Creates strong off-balancing opportunities
Allows attacks from multiple angles
Transitions easily into other open guard systems
Very effective at slowing aggressive opponents
Works well against larger opponents using leverage
Strong sweeping potential from both seated and supine positions
cons: Requires strong sleeve grip endurance
Only effective in Gi due to sleeve grips
Grip breaks can quickly collapse the guard
Standing opponents can create passing pressure
Energy intensive if grips are constantly contested
Can be vulnerable to stack pressure when legs are extended
Requires good hip mobility and timing
tips: Maintain sleeve grips at all times — they are the foundation of spider guard
Keep your hips mobile to constantly adjust angles
Push with one leg while pulling with grips to create off-balance
Transition between single spider and double spider dynamically
Use spider guard to set up triangles, omoplatas, and sweeps
Do not extend both legs fully at the same time unless controlling balance
Combine spider with lasso or collar sleeve guards for stronger control
Attack immediately when your opponent loses posture
Break their balance before attempting sweeps

sweeps.name: Spider Guard Sweep
sweeps.description: Push one bicep while pulling the sleeve and angling the hips to sweep the opponent sideways.

sweeps.name: Balloon Sweep
sweeps.description: Use sleeve grips and leg extension to elevate the opponent and sweep them over your head.

sweeps.name: Overhead Sweep
sweeps.description: Pull the opponent forward and extend your legs to flip them over your head.

sweeps.name: Tripod Sweep (Spider Setup)
sweeps.description: Control the sleeve while placing a foot on the hip and another behind the ankle to off-balance and sweep.

sweeps.name: Transition to Lasso
sweeps.description: Transition into lasso guard and use rotational pressure to sweep.

sweeps.name: Transition to De la Riva
sweeps.description: Transition one leg to De La Riva hook and attack sweeping angles.

passes.name: Grip Break and Torreando Pass
passes.description: Break sleeve grips and immediately circle around the legs using the toreando pass.

passes.name: Leg Drag Pass
passes.description: Drag one leg across the body after breaking spider hooks to pass.

passes.name: Stack Pass
passes.description: Drive the opponent’s knees toward their chest to collapse the spider structure and pass.

passes.name: Knee Slice Pass
passes.description: Break sleeve grips and slice your knee through the middle to pass.

passes.name: Double Under Stack Pass
passes.description: Secure both legs under your arms and stack to neutralize spider hooks.

submissions.name: Triangle Choke
submissions.description: Use sleeve control and leg movement to trap the opponent’s neck and arm.

submissions.name: Omoplata
submissions.description: Attack the shoulder when the opponent’s posture breaks during spider control.

submissions.name: Armbar
submissions.description: Swing your leg over the opponent’s arm when they attempt to posture or defend.

submissions.name: Triangle to Armbar Combo
submissions.description: Transition from triangle attempt to armbar if the opponent defends.

guards.name: De La Riva 🪝
guards.description: The De La Riva guard is an open guard position named after Ricardo De La Riva. The bottom player hooks the outside of the opponent’s lead leg with their own leg while controlling grips to off-balance, create angles, and attack sweeps, back takes, or transitions to other guards.
execution: From open guard, identify the opponent’s lead leg
Hook your outside leg behind their near leg with your shin wrapping around the calf
Use your foot to hook the inner thigh or hip to maintain tension
Control the opponent’s far sleeve, collar, or ankle depending on the variation
Use your free leg to control their hip, bicep, or far leg
Keep your hips mobile so you can pivot and create angles
Maintain tension with your hook to disrupt their balance
Use grips and hooks together to pull your opponent forward
Adjust your hips constantly to avoid being flattened
pros: Excellent guard for off-balancing standing opponents
Multiple sweep and back take opportunities
Transitions easily into modern guards like berimbolo and X-guard
Effective against standing guard passers
Creates strong asymmetrical control
Works in both Gi and No-Gi with grip adjustments
Allows dynamic movement and guard retention
cons: Requires good timing and coordination
Beginners may struggle with hook positioning
Backstep passes can counter the hook
Can be flattened if grips are lost
Requires constant movement and grip fighting
Less effective if opponent forces half guard
Hook tension can fatigue the legs over time
tips: Keep tension in your De La Riva hook to control the opponent’s leg
Use your free leg actively to frame or off-balance
Control the far sleeve or ankle to prevent posture
Angle your hips rather than staying flat on your back
Chain attacks together to keep the opponent reacting
Practice transitions into berimbolo and back takes
Learn to switch between DLR and reverse DLR
Use collar drags to attack the back
Stay mobile to avoid pressure passes
Master basic sweeps before attempting advanced inversions

sweeps.name: Classic De La Riva Sweep
sweeps.description: Use the hook and sleeve or collar grip to off-balance and sweep the opponent to the side.

sweeps.name: Berimbolo
sweeps.description: Invert underneath the opponent to take the back or complete a sweep.

sweeps.name: Babybolo
sweeps.description: A simplified berimbolo-style back take where you use De La Riva control to off-balance your opponent, invert underneath, and rotate to expose the back without requiring full inversion depth.

sweeps.name: Kiss of the Dragon
sweeps.description: Invert under the opponent’s leg and rotate around to take the back.

sweeps.name: Sit-Up Sweep (DLR Variation)
sweeps.description: Come up to a seated position while maintaining the hook and drive forward for a sweep.

sweeps.name: Balloon Sweep
sweeps.description: Use grips and leg extension to elevate the opponent and sweep them overhead.

sweeps.name: DLR to X-Guard Sweep
sweeps.description: Transition to X-guard and complete the sweep from the leg entanglement.

sweeps.name: DLR to Single Leg X Sweep
sweeps.description: Drop underneath the opponent to enter single leg X and sweep.

sweeps.name: Collar Drag to Back Take
sweeps.description: Use a collar drag to spin behind the opponent and secure back control.

sweeps.name: DLR to Matrix
sweeps.description: From De La Riva guard, invert under your opponent while maintaining the De La Riva hook and grip control, rotating beneath them to expose their back and enter the Matrix position for a back take or sweeping finish.

sweeps.name: DLR Wrestle-Up Sweep
sweeps.description: From De La Riva guard, off-balance your opponent backward, release the guard, and come up to while maintaining control to wrestle up into top position.

passes.name: Back Step Pass
passes.description: Step backward and around the De La Riva hook to clear it and pass.

passes.name: Knee Slice Pass
passes.description: Clear the hook and drive the knee across the opponent’s legs to pass.

passes.name: Leg Drag Pass
passes.description: Control the opponent’s leg and drag it across their body to pass.

passes.name: Toreando Pass
passes.description: Control the legs and circle around quickly to bypass the guard.

passes.name: Stack Pass
passes.description: Drive the opponent’s knees toward their chest to collapse their guard structure.

passes.name: Long Step Pass
passes.description: Step the hooked leg far back to break the hook and circle around the guard.

passes.name: Headquarters Pass
passes.description: Control the opponent’s legs from headquarters position to prevent hooks.

passes.name: Leg Weave
passes.description: Thread your arm between the opponent’s legs to weave and staple them together, applying shoulder pressure while circling to the side to collapse their guard and pass.

passes.name: X Pass
passes.description: Control one of the opponent’s legs while stepping over with your opposite leg in an X-shaped configuration. Apply chest and shoulder pressure, clear their hooks, and drive across to establish side control or top position.

submissions.name: Triangle Choke
submissions.description: Create an angle from De La Riva and trap the opponent’s neck and arm.

submissions.name: Omoplata
submissions.description: Swing your leg over the opponent’s arm to isolate the shoulder.

submissions.name: Armbar
submissions.description: Attack the arm when the opponent posts or loses posture.

submissions.name: Loop Choke
submissions.description: Use collar grips to trap the opponent’s neck when they pressure forward.

submissions.name: Straight Ankle Lock
submissions.description: Transition into leg entanglements and attack the ankle.

submissions.name: Estima Lock
submissions.description: Advanced foot lock attack available from specific De La Riva entries.

submissions.name: Kimura
submissions.description: Attack the shoulder when the opponent posts an arm while defending sweeps.

submissions.name: Back Take to Chokes
submissions.description: Use berimbolo or other transitions to take the back and finish with chokes.

submissions.name: Caio Terra Leg Lock
submissions.description: From De La Riva or leg entanglement positions, isolate the opponent’s leg and attack the knee or ankle with a controlled leg lock while maintaining your hook and base.

submissions.name: Armbar Shotgun
submissions.description: From a high-angle De La Riva setup, swing your leg over the opponent’s arm and torso in a fast, explosive motion to finish a tight armbar.

guards.name: Butterfly Guard 🦋
guards.description: Butterfly guard is a seated open guard where the bottom player places hooks under the opponent’s thighs while sitting upright. This allows the guard player to elevate, off-balance, and sweep the opponent while maintaining strong upper body control.
execution: Sit upright with your knees bent and feet inside your opponent’s legs
Place your feet as hooks under the opponent’s inner thighs
Keep your hooks active with your feet flexed
Stay upright with good posture and keep your chest close to your opponent
Control the upper body using sleeve, collar, wrist, or overhook grips
Fight for underhooks whenever possible
Stay on the balls of your feet ready to elevate
Keep your hips mobile to create angles for sweeps
Use frames with your arms to manage distance and prevent pressure
pros: Powerful and high-percentage sweeps
Effective in both Gi and No-Gi
Works well for smaller practitioners
Natural position for arm drags and back takes
Easy to transition into wrestling stand-ups
Strong entries into leg entanglements
Highly dynamic and mobile guard
cons: Requires good timing and balance
Back exposure is possible if posture is lost
Can be flattened by strong pressure
Crossface pressure can collapse the guard
Requires active grip fighting and movement
Less effective if opponent maintains strong posture
tips: Prioritize getting at least one strong underhook
Stay upright rather than lying flat on your back
Constantly off-balance your opponent before sweeping
Chain sweeps together if the first attempt fails
Use arm drags to attack the back
Transition to half guard if pressure becomes too heavy
Use butterfly guard to wrestle up when opportunities appear
Practice transitions into X-guard and single leg X
Maintain inside position with your hooks

sweeps.name: Butterfly Sweep (Classic)
sweeps.description: Elevate the opponent with one hook while controlling the upper body to sweep them over.

sweeps.name: Sit-Up Sweep
sweeps.description: Drive forward while elevating with the hook and come up on top.

sweeps.name: Arm Drag to Back Take
sweeps.description: Drag the opponent’s arm across and move behind them to secure back control.

sweeps.name: Wrestle-Up Sweep
sweeps.description: Use butterfly guard to come up to your feet and finish a takedown.

sweeps.name: Butterfly to X-Guard Sweep
sweeps.description: Transition to X-guard underneath the opponent and sweep from there.

sweeps.name: Butterfly to Single Leg X Sweep
sweeps.description: Drop underneath to enter single leg X and complete the sweep.

passes.name: Crossface Pressure Pass
passes.description: Apply strong crossface pressure to flatten the guard player and clear hooks.

passes.name: Body Lock Pass
passes.description: Lock your hands around the opponent’s torso and drive forward to flatten them.

passes.name: Sprawl and Smash
passes.description: Sprawl your hips back when the opponent tries to elevate and drive pressure forward.

passes.name: Leg Drag Pass
passes.description: Drag the opponent’s leg across the body to control and pass.

passes.name: Knee Slice Pass
passes.description: Slice your knee across the opponent’s legs while controlling the upper body.

passes.name: Stack Pass
passes.description: Drive the opponent’s knees toward their chest to collapse the guard.

passes.name: Backstep Pass
passes.description: Step back and around when the opponent elevates for sweeps.

submissions.name: Guillotine Choke
submissions.description: Attack the neck when the opponent drives forward into the guard.

submissions.name: Arm-in Guillotine
submissions.description: Trap the opponent’s arm inside the guillotine to increase pressure.

submissions.name: Kimura
submissions.description: Attack the shoulder when the opponent posts an arm or defends sweeps.

submissions.name: Triangle Choke
submissions.description: Create an angle and trap the opponent’s neck and arm with your legs.

submissions.name: Straight Ankle Lock
submissions.description: Enter leg entanglements from butterfly guard and attack the ankle.

submissions.name: Front Headlock Chokes
submissions.description: Control the opponent’s head and attack guillotine-style chokes.

submissions.name: Loop Choke
submissions.description: Use collar grips to trap the opponent’s neck when they pressure forward.

guards.name: Half Guard 🛡️
guards.description: Half guard is a position where the bottom player controls one of the opponent’s legs between both of their legs. It is a versatile guard that allows for sweeps, back takes, and transitions into other guards such as deep half, knee shield, and butterfly half.
execution: Lie on your side with your opponent’s leg trapped between your legs
Lock your legs around their leg to control it
Fight for the underhook on the far side whenever possible
Keep your inside knee blocking their hip or thigh
Stay on your side rather than flat on your back
Use frames on their shoulder or neck to prevent crossface pressure
Keep your head active to avoid being flattened
Control their trapped leg with your bottom leg and outside hook
Use your hips to create angles and attack sweeps
pros: Strong recovery position when guard is almost passed
Works well in both Gi and No-Gi
Effective against larger opponents
Multiple sweep and back take opportunities
Transitions into deep half, knee shield, or butterfly half
Allows the bottom player to slow the pace
Good entry point for leg entanglements
cons: Can be smashed by strong pressure if flattened
Crossface pressure can neutralize attacks
Requires constant battle for the underhook
Harder to attack if opponent maintains strong base
Risk of being passed if guard is inactive
Requires good timing for sweeps
Leg fatigue can occur when maintaining control
tips: Winning the underhook battle is critical
Never allow your opponent to flatten you
Use a knee shield to create space when needed
Transition between half guard variations to stay active
Practice coming up to your elbow or hand when sweeping
Combine half guard with butterfly hooks for additional control
Use frames to manage distance and prevent crossface
Focus on controlling the trapped leg before attacking
Chain sweeps together when your opponent defends
Use deep half guard as an alternative attack option

sweeps.name: Old School Sweep
sweeps.description: Use the underhook and control of the opponent’s leg to sweep them backward.

sweeps.name: Deep Half Sweep (Waiter Sweep)
sweeps.description: Move underneath the opponent in deep half guard and lift their leg to sweep.

sweeps.name: Homer Simpson Sweep
sweeps.description: From deep half guard, roll underneath to reverse the opponent.

sweeps.name: Knee Tap Sweep
sweeps.description: Control the opponent’s far knee while coming up to sweep them sideways.

sweeps.name: Plan B Sweep
sweeps.description: Roll underneath the opponent when they defend deep half guard.

sweeps.name: Lockdown Sweep
sweeps.description: Use the lockdown to extend the opponent’s leg and create sweeping opportunities.

sweeps.name: Back Take from Half Guard
sweeps.description: Use the underhook and angle to come behind the opponent and take the back.

sweeps.name: John Wayne Sweep
sweeps.description: From half guard, off-balance the opponent forward while controlling a sleeve and leg. Sit up and rotate your body, using your hook to roll the opponent over sideways and establish top control.

sweeps.name: Dog Fight Sweep
sweeps.description: From half guard, secure underhooks and control the opponent’s posture. Bridge and roll to one side while pulling their upper body, using leverage to sweep them onto their back and achieve dominant position.

passes.name: Crossface Pressure Pass
passes.description: Apply heavy crossface pressure to flatten the opponent and free the trapped leg.

passes.name: Knee Slice Pass
passes.description: Slice your knee through the half guard while controlling the upper body.

passes.name: Underhook Pass
passes.description: Win the underhook battle and drive forward to clear the guard.

passes.name: Backstep Pass
passes.description: Step backward and around the half guard to free your leg.

passes.name: Hip Switch Pass
passes.description: Switch your hips to free your trapped leg and clear the guard.

submissions.name: Kimura
submissions.description: Attack the opponent’s far arm using a kimura grip.

submissions.name: Guillotine Choke
submissions.description: Catch the opponent’s neck when they drive forward into half guard.

submissions.name: Arm-in Guillotine
submissions.description: Trap the opponent’s arm inside the guillotine for additional control.

submissions.name: D'Arce Choke
submissions.description: From half guard, thread your arm under the opponent’s neck and through the near arm, locking your hands together in a figure-four grip. Squeeze while keeping chest pressure to finish the choke.

submissions.name: Ezekiel Choke
submissions.description: From half guard, slip one arm across the opponent’s neck while securing your sleeve or wrist with the other hand. Press your forearm or gi material into their throat and use chest pressure to complete the choke.

guards.name: X-Guard ✖️
guards.description: X-Guard is a dynamic sweeping guard where you position yourself under a standing opponent with your legs forming an 'X'. One leg hooks behind their near knee (bottom hook) while the other controls their far hip or thigh (top hook), creating elevation and off-balancing opportunities for sweeps and transitions.
execution: Start from open guard, half guard, or butterfly guard
Establish one foot on their far hip or thigh (top hook)
Thread your other leg behind their near knee (bottom hook)
Pinch knees to control their trapped leg and form the 'X'
Control their sleeve, wrist, or underhook to break posture
Keep hips elevated and active, never flat on the mat
Off-balance opponent forward, backward, or diagonally
Use bottom hook to lift and top hook to steer their leg
Transition to sweeps, single leg X, or back takes as needed
pros: Extremely powerful sweeping position
Works effectively in both Gi and No-Gi
Great for smaller practitioners against bigger opponents
Smooth transitions to back takes and leg entanglements
Excellent for standing up in scrambles
Multiple sweep angles available
Difficult for opponent to establish base or pressure
cons: Requires precise timing and entry
Vulnerable to guard jumps, knee bars, or leg entanglements if sloppy
Requires hip mobility and core engagement
Challenging against opponents with a very wide base
Requires constant movement and active off-balancing
Can be passed if opponent drops weight correctly or collapses hips
tips: Master the entry from half guard and butterfly guard
Always control the upper body and pull opponent forward
Use the top hook to steer while bottom hook elevates
Chain X-guard with single leg X for more options
Keep moving and off-balancing, don’t stay static
Study Marcelo Garcia and Leandro Lo for system mastery
Learn to stand up from X-guard in scrambles
Focus on kuzushi (off-balancing) before sweeping
Practice timing sweeps with opponent’s weight shifts
Combine X-guard with leg entanglement entries for No-Gi

sweeps.name: X-Guard Sweep (Classic)
sweeps.description: Elevate with bottom hook and tilt opponent diagonally to sweep.

sweeps.name: X-Guard to Back Take
sweeps.description: Use X-guard control to off-balance and insert hooks to take the back.

sweeps.name: Technical Stand-up
sweeps.description: Come up to your feet from X-guard while controlling opponent’s leg.

sweeps.name: Elevator Sweep
sweeps.description: Use bottom hook like a lever to lift and sweep opponent sideways.

sweeps.name: Overhead Sweep
sweeps.description: Pull opponent over your head when posture is broken and top hook guides.

sweeps.name: Single Leg X Transition Sweep
sweeps.description: Transition from X-guard to single leg X and sweep by controlling ankle and leg.

sweeps.name: Reverse X Sweep
sweeps.description: Shift to reverse X-guard and sweep opponent using new angle of control.

sweeps.name: Crab Ride to Back
sweeps.description: Transition from X-guard to crab ride to take the back efficiently.

passes.name: Sit Down Pass
passes.description: Sit down on opponent’s bottom hook to break X-guard and clear leg.

passes.name: Wide Base Pass
passes.description: Widen base and post to prevent elevation and escape X-guard hooks.

passes.name: Leg Drag
passes.description: Control opponent’s bottom leg and drag to side to pass.

passes.name: Smash Pass
passes.description: Drive weight down to collapse X-guard structure and pin opponent.

passes.name: Backstep Pass
passes.description: Step back around X-guard hooks to free leg and pass.

passes.name: Stack Pass
passes.description: Stack opponent on shoulders to break X-guard and control posture.

passes.name: Knee Slice
passes.description: Clear hooks and slice knee across to side control.

passes.name: Pressure Pass
passes.description: Use chest and hip pressure to flatten opponent and clear X-guard.

submissions.name: Straight Ankle Lock
submissions.description: Attack opponent’s ankle from single leg X or X-guard leg entanglement.

submissions.name: Knee Bar
submissions.description: Isolate and attack trapped knee when opponent posts or backsteps.

submissions.name: Heel Hook (No-Gi)
submissions.description: Enter heel hook from single leg X or reverse X transitions.

submissions.name: Back Take to Chokes
submissions.description: Take the back from X-guard and finish with rear naked or bow and arrow choke.

guards.name: Single Leg X 🦵
guards.description: Single Leg X (also known as Ashi Garami) is a powerful open guard position where you isolate one of your opponent's legs between yours. Your inside leg hooks behind their knee while your outside foot controls their far hip, allowing strong off-balancing, sweeping, and direct transitions into leg lock attacks.
execution: Enter from open guard, butterfly guard, half guard, or X-guard
Isolate one of your opponent's legs between your hips
Place your outside foot firmly on their far hip for distance and control
Thread your inside leg tightly behind their near knee (primary hook)
Pinch knees to control their trapped leg
Control their ankle, heel, or pant grip (Gi) to prevent escape
Keep your hips off the mat and aligned under their center of gravity
Off-balance them by pushing the hip while pulling the ankle
Stay ready to sweep, wrestle up, or transition to full X-guard or other leg entanglements
pros: Core position in modern leg lock systems
Highly effective in both Gi and No-Gi
Excellent sweeping mechanics with minimal strength required
Strong control over opponent's mobility
Great for smaller grapplers against larger opponents
Natural transitions to X-guard, reverse X, and back takes
Direct access to high-percentage leg attacks
cons: Exposure to counter leg locks if positioning is sloppy
Can be neutralized if opponent clears knee line
Vulnerable if hips are flat or disconnected
Requires good understanding of leg lock defense
Can be smashed if opponent drops heavy pressure correctly
Less effective against opponents who immediately sit or backstep
tips: Always control the ankle or heel — never allow free rotation
Push the hip while pulling the ankle to create kuzushi
Keep your inside hook tight and active behind their knee
Angle your hips slightly to prevent stacking pressure
Chain SLX with full X-guard for layered attacks
Practice technical stand-up sweeps for high-percentage finishes
Clear their free foot when attacking leg locks
Learn proper knee line control before attacking heel hooks
Transition immediately if opponent postures strongly
Study modern ashi garami systems for deeper understanding

sweeps.name: Single Leg X Sweep (Classic)
sweeps.description: Push the hip while pulling the ankle to tilt opponent backward.

sweeps.name: Technical Stand-Up Sweep
sweeps.description: Come up to standing while maintaining ankle control for a takedown.

sweeps.name: Overhead Sweep
sweeps.description: Elevate and guide opponent forward over your head when posture breaks.

sweeps.name: Transition to X-Guard Sweep
sweeps.description: Insert second hook to establish full X-guard and sweep.

guards.name: Deep Half Guard 🕳️
guards.description: Deep Half Guard is a bottom position where you scoot deep underneath your opponent's hips from half guard, controlling one of their legs while your body is almost entirely beneath them. It neutralizes their weight and creates powerful sweeping and back-take opportunities.
execution: Start from standard half guard with an underhook or overhook
Scoot your hips deep under your opponent's center of gravity
Control their far leg tightly with your arms and legs
Get your head past their hip line on the far side
Keep your body perpendicular under them, aligned with their center
Use your top arm to hug thigh, belt, or waist for control
Frame with your bottom arm to prevent crossface or sprawl
Keep your inside half guard hook to block stepping over
Maintain tight contact — minimize space to control posture
Stay ready to sweep, transition to back, or attack legs
pros: Neutralizes top player's weight and pressure effectively
High-percentage sweeps from a defensive position
Effective for smaller grapplers against larger opponents
Allows initiative from traditionally defensive guard
Smooth transitions to back takes, single legs, and X-guard
Works well in both Gi and No-Gi
Difficult for opponent to submit you directly from deep half
cons: Vulnerable to d'arce, anaconda, and guillotine during entries
Can be flattened if not deep enough
Requires precise timing to avoid being smashed
Opponent can crossface to prevent depth
Risk of back exposure if sweeps fail
Guillotine choke risk if head placement is poor
Experienced top players can stall by sprawling and waiting
tips: Commit fully when entering — halfway is unsafe
Protect your neck during entry to avoid chokes
Hug their thigh tightly for maximum control
Use high-percentage sweeps like Homer Simpson and Waiter Sweep
Study Jeff Glover and Bernardo Faria’s deep half systems
Keep hips moving and adjust angles constantly
Use deep half as a rest position — opponent carries own weight
Drill entries from half guard and quarter guard
Chain sweeps — if one fails, immediately go to the next
Always track opponent’s free leg and posture

guards.name: Rubber Guard 🧘
guards.description: Rubber Guard is a closed guard system popularized by Eddie Bravo in the 10th Planet Jiu-Jitsu system. It uses a high leg over the opponent’s shoulder combined with an overhook to break posture and control the upper body without relying on gi grips. It is especially effective in No-Gi and MMA settings.
execution: Start from closed guard and break opponent’s posture first
Establish a strong overhook on one arm
Bring your same-side leg high over their shoulder
Grab your shin (Mission Control position)
Pull their posture down using your leg instead of your arms
Keep your foot above their shoulder line for maximum control
Angle your hips slightly toward the overhook side
Use your free hand to control their far wrist (Zombie control)
Progress through Rubber Guard positions (New York, Chill Dog, etc.)
Attack submissions while maintaining posture control
pros: Excellent posture control without gi grips
Frees hands for submissions while leg maintains control
Highly effective in No-Gi and MMA
Strong submission chains once posture is broken
Difficult for opponent to strike effectively in MMA
Unique attack system unfamiliar to many opponents
Allows tight upper body isolation
cons: Requires high hip flexibility
Risk of knee strain if forced
Steep learning curve with layered system
Can be stacked if posture control is lost
Less effective if opponent stays very upright early
Limited sweeping emphasis compared to other guards
Energy intensive if grips are not secure
tips: Always break posture before entering Mission Control
Keep your foot high — below shoulder line loses control
Control the far wrist to prevent posture recovery
Angle your hips — never stay flat under opponent
Focus on submission chains rather than single attacks
Master Mission Control before advancing to New York or Chill Dog
Use omoplata as a primary high-percentage attack
Transition to clamp guard if opponent postures
Study Eddie Bravo’s 10th Planet system structure
Do not force flexibility — build it progressively

guards.name: Reverse De La Riva 🔄
guards.description: Reverse De La Riva (RDLR) is an open guard where you hook your outside leg around your opponent’s lead leg from the inside, with your shin positioned behind their knee. It is a key modern guard used for berimbolo entries, back takes, and transitions to leg entanglements and sweeping systems.
execution: Start from open guard when opponent steps one leg forward
Insert your outside leg behind their lead knee from the inside
Keep your shin tight behind their knee to maintain hook tension
Place your free foot on their hip or far thigh for distance control
Angle your hips toward the hooked leg side
Control sleeve, collar (Gi), or ankle depending on rule set
Keep your hips mobile and slightly off the mat
Prevent crossface pressure by maintaining angle and distance
Be ready to invert, transition to SLX/X-guard, or attack back takes
pros: Gateway to berimbolo and modern back takes
Excellent counter to knee slice passing
Smooth transitions to X-guard, SLX, and 50/50
Strong off-balancing potential with minimal strength
Effective in Gi and adaptable to No-Gi
Creates constant dilemmas between sweep and back exposure
Difficult to pressure if angle is maintained
cons: Requires comfort with inversion mechanics
Vulnerable to leg drag if hook becomes loose
Can be smashed by strong crossface pressure
Backstep may expose you to leg lock battles
Steep learning curve for berimbolo timing
Requires hip mobility and angle awareness
Less intuitive for beginners
tips: Keep constant tension in your RDLR hook
Angle your hips — never stay flat under opponent
Use RDLR primarily as a transition hub
Master berimbolo and kiss of the dragon entries
Control their far hip or sleeve to prevent backstep
Switch to SLX if they square up
Transition to deep half if they drop heavy pressure
Drill inversion mechanics safely and progressively
Combine standard DLR and RDLR for layered guard play
Anticipate leg drag and counter early

guards.name: Collar Sleeve Guard 👔
guards.description: Collar Sleeve Guard is an open guard position built on a deep cross-collar grip and same-side sleeve control. With one foot on the hip and the other on the bicep, it creates strong distance management, off-balancing ability, and powerful angle-based attacks. It is one of the most effective attacking guard systems in Gi Jiu-Jitsu.
execution: Start in open guard with opponent standing or kneeling.
Secure a deep cross-collar grip with one hand.
Control the same-side sleeve with your opposite hand.
Place your collar-side foot on their same-side hip.
Place your sleeve-side foot on their bicep.
Angle your hips toward the collar-grip side.
Maintain push-pull tension between grips and feet.
Constantly off-balance before attacking.
Attack only when you’ve broken their posture or base.
pros: Elite distance control.
Strong posture breaking with collar grip.
High-percentage triangle and omoplata entries.
Natural angle creation for attacks.
Flows seamlessly into lasso and spider guard.
Works well against standing opponents.
cons: Requires strong grip endurance.
Loses power if collar grip is shallow.
Can be leg dragged if hip foot is misplaced.
Stack pressure is dangerous if angles are lost.
Less effective once opponent closes distance.
tips: The collar grip must be deep — shallow grip kills the guard.
Always keep your hip foot active to manage distance.
Pull sleeve across center line before attacking triangle.
Never attack square — create angle first.
If they pressure in, transition to lasso immediately.
Use constant kuzushi before committing to sweeps.
Re-grip immediately if collar control is broken.
Think angle, not force.

guards.name: Z-Guard (Knee Shield) 🛡️
guards.description: Z-Guard is a structured half guard variation where the top knee forms a strong shield across the opponent’s chest or shoulder line while the bottom leg traps their leg. Combined with proper hip angle and inside control (underhook or frames), it creates distance, prevents pressure, and opens direct paths to sweeps, back takes, leg entries, and upper-body attacks. It is a cornerstone of modern half guard systems in both Gi and No-Gi.
execution: Start from half guard with one of their legs trapped.
Insert your top knee across their chest or shoulder line as a shield.
Keep your shin angled diagonally — not flat across the belly.
Maintain strong bottom-leg engagement on their trapped leg.
Angle your hips toward the shield side — never flat on your back.
Establish an underhook or strong collar/wrist control.
Use the knee shield to manage distance and prevent chest-to-chest pressure.
Fight for inside position before attempting sweeps.
Transition immediately if the shield begins to collapse.
pros: Excellent distance and pressure management.
Prevents crossface and heavy chest pressure.
Strong entry hub to deep half, dogfight, leg entanglements, and Choi bar attacks.
Works effectively in Gi and No-Gi.
Highly adaptable to modern half guard systems.
Allows recovery when guard is partially passed.
cons: Loses effectiveness if hips are flattened.
Collapsible if opponent wins crossface and underhook.
Knee slice pressure directly targets the shield.
Requires active inside control — passive shield gets smashed.
Backstep passing can bypass the structure.
Poor angle control kills offensive potential.
tips: Shield high on chest — not low on stomach.
Your underhook matters more than the knee shield.
Never stay flat — angle toward the shield side.
If crossfaced, immediately recover inside frames.
If shield collapses, transition to deep half or wrestle-up.
Use the knee shield to create movement, not to stall.
Combine kimura grip and Choi bar for powerful upper-body chains.
Think inside position first, sweep second.

guards.name: Octopus Guard 🐙
guards.description: Octopus Guard is a sit-up half guard variation where you attach tightly to your opponent using a strong overhook and deep far-side underhook. By wrapping around their torso and staying glued chest-to-chest, you create powerful lateral off-balancing and direct paths to sweeps and back takes. Popularized by Eduardo Telles, it converts half guard into an aggressive, angle-driven attacking position.
execution: Start from half guard with their leg trapped.
Sit up onto your elbow and then your hand — do not stay flat.
Secure a tight overhook on the near-side arm.
Thread your far arm deep for an underhook across their back.
Glue your chest to their ribs — eliminate space.
Keep your half guard hook on the overhook side.
Drive your head under their jaw or into their far armpit.
Shift your hips laterally to off-balance them.
Commit fully to angles — hesitation kills the position.
pros: Turns half guard into a strong offensive position.
Direct path to back exposure.
Excellent for lateral sweeps.
Neutralizes heavy chest pressure by sitting up.
Highly effective against opponents who drive forward.
Works in both Gi and No-Gi.
Unfamiliar position for many training partners.
cons: Timing-dependent — slow entry gets you flattened.
Neck exposure during sit-up phase.
Requires strong upper-body connection.
Vulnerable to strong whizzer pressure.
Can be shut down if opponent wins crossface early.
Less effective if you remain square.
tips: Commit fully to the sit-up — half-entry fails.
The overhook is your anchor — keep it tight.
Head position determines leverage — win head height.
Your chest must stay glued to their ribs.
Attack sideways, not backward.
If they pressure into you, redirect and sweep.
If they pull away, chase the back.
Chain directly into dogfight when space opens.
Never stay static — constant angle adjustment.
Think attachment, not guard retention.

guards.name: Worm Guard 🪱
guards.description: Worm Guard is a lapel-based De La Riva guard system created by Keenan Cornelius. By extracting and wrapping your opponent’s lapel around their leg, you create a powerful tether that controls distance, restricts movement, and destabilizes their base. Worm Guard is primarily a sweeping and back-taking system that dominates modern Gi competition.
execution: Start from De La Riva or seated open guard.
Pull out your opponent’s far-side lapel.
Feed the lapel underneath their near leg.
Thread the lapel behind their leg and secure it with your bottom hand.
Establish a De La Riva hook on the same leg being wrapped.
Keep constant tension on the lapel — no slack.
Use your free hand to control their far sleeve or collar.
Maintain outside hip angle — never flat.
Use the tether to break posture and disrupt their base before sweeping.
pros: Extremely difficult to pass once tension is established.
Controls opponent’s mobility through lapel tether.
High-percentage sweep system.
Strong funnel to back takes.
Forces opponent into predictable reactions.
Highly effective in Gi competition.
Transitions directly to squid, ringworm, and lapel lasso systems.
cons: Gi-only position.
Requires time and grip awareness to establish.
Can be shut down early if lapel is controlled.
Grip fatigue over long matches.
Complex system with specific reactions.
Stalling risk if sweeps are not initiated actively.
tips: Speed in lapel extraction is critical.
Never allow slack in the lapel tether.
Angle outside — do not stay square.
DLR hook and lapel tension must work together.
If they try to strip lapel, attack immediately.
Control their far sleeve to prevent cartwheel or spin escapes.
Use tension to create kuzushi before sweeping.
Think sweep-first, submission-second.

guards.name: K Guard 🔑
guards.description: K Guard is a modern leg entanglement entry system popularized by Lachlan Giles. From seated or open guard, one shin frames across the opponent’s thigh while the far leg threads behind their far leg, forming a 'K' shape. The position is designed to expose the secondary leg and create direct entries into inside sankaku, backside 50/50, and outside ashi. It is a cornerstone of contemporary No-Gi leg lock systems.
execution: Start from seated, butterfly, or open guard.
Place your near-side shin across their lead thigh.
Thread your far leg behind their far leg at knee level.
Keep your hips angled — do not lie flat.
Control their near ankle or heel to prevent stepping out.
Maintain shin pressure to manage distance.
Off-balance them slightly before entering.
Expose their far leg by lifting and rotating under.
Transition immediately into inside sankaku, backside 50/50, or outside ashi.
pros: Elite entry into inside heel hook positions.
Difficult to recognize until entry is already happening.
Pairs naturally with butterfly and seated guard.
Strong against standing opponents.
Allows immediate transition to backside 50/50.
Low upper-body risk when performed correctly.
cons: Requires deep understanding of leg entanglement hierarchy.
Timing-sensitive — late entries get smashed.
Less effective in Gi due to grip counters.
Exposes your own legs to counter leg locks.
Limited control if opponent drops hips early.
Not ideal against kneeling heavy pressure players.
tips: Your shin frame controls distance — keep it heavy.
Hide your threading leg until ready to enter.
Enter decisively — do not pause in K Guard.
Primary target is inside sankaku.
If they step back, enter backside 50/50.
Control the ankle before rotating under.
Angle your hips before exposing the heel line.
Use K Guard as a gateway, not a resting position.

guards.name: 50/50 Guard ⚖️
guards.description: 50/50 Guard is a symmetrical leg entanglement position where both players have identical hooks on each other's legs, mirroring one another. Each player threads one leg inside and wraps around the opponent's opposite leg, creating an even or 'fifty-fifty' position. It is a cornerstone of modern leg lock systems and is also used strategically for sweeps and advantages in Gi competition.
execution: Start from open guard, single leg X, K Guard, or a scramble
Thread your inside leg between your opponent's legs
Wrap your leg around their far leg from the outside
Hook your foot behind their knee or thigh on the far side
Your opponent will have a mirrored hook on your leg
Control their far ankle or heel with your hands
Keep your hips close to their leg and stay tight
Use your free leg and arms to maintain balance and control
Stay on your side or slightly elevated — never flat on your back
pros: Foundation of modern heel hook and leg lock systems
Symmetrical position offers equal attacking opportunities
Works extremely well in No-Gi grappling and ADCC rules
Excellent for accumulating advantages in Gi competition (sweeps)
Difficult for opponent to disengage once entangled
Creates a 50/50 submission race that rewards technique
Natural transition point from many guards (DLR, K Guard, SLX)
cons: Can lead to stalling matches — both players may be stuck
Symmetrical nature means your opponent has equal attacks
Penalized or disliked in some Gi rulesets for stalling potential
Requires strong leg lock knowledge to be effective offensively
Can be difficult to sweep from without leg lock threat
Exposes your own legs to counter attacks at all times
Can become a positional stalemate without finishing ability
tips: Study Gordon Ryan and the Danaher Death Squad for elite 50/50 systems
The inside position (inside sankaku) is more dominant — fight for it
Always control your opponent's heel or ankle — never let it float
Use the 50/50 sweep to come on top and then attack leg locks
Learn the honey hole / inside sankaku transition from 50/50
Practice escaping 50/50 as much as attacking — it goes both ways
Use the backside 50/50 for a more dominant heel hook angle
In Gi, use 50/50 sweeps to score advantages and points strategically
Don't just sit in 50/50 — actively work to improve your angle
Chain heel hook attacks with toe holds and knee bars from 50/50

guards.name: Lasso Guard 🪢
guards.description: Lasso Guard is a powerful open guard position where you wrap your leg around your opponent's arm from the outside, threading your foot through and hooking it behind their shoulder or bicep. Combined with a sleeve grip on the lassoed arm, it creates a strong tether that controls their posture and movement. It is a staple of modern Gi guard play, often used alongside spider and collar sleeve guards.
execution: Start from open guard with your opponent standing or on their knees
Grip their same-side sleeve with a strong pistol grip
Thread your leg over their arm from the outside
Wrap your shin behind their arm and hook your foot behind their shoulder or bicep
Keep the lasso leg deep — your foot should be past their shoulder line
Use your other foot on their hip or bicep for distance control
Maintain constant tension on the sleeve grip — never let it slacken
Keep your hips off the mat and angled toward the lasso side
Use your free hand to grip their collar, belt, or far sleeve for additional control
pros: Extremely powerful posture and distance control
Shuts down many common guard passing strategies
Multiple high-percentage sweeps available
Great for setting up triangles and omoplatas
Pairs naturally with spider guard and collar sleeve guard
Effective against both standing and kneeling opponents
Allows you to control much larger and stronger opponents
cons: Only works in the Gi — requires sleeve grips
Demands strong grip endurance over long matches
Can be passed if opponent strips the lasso grip
Vulnerable to backstep passes if the lasso is shallow
Requires flexibility in the hips for deep lasso
Can be stacked if opponent drives forward aggressively
Limited effectiveness if opponent refuses to engage and stays at distance
tips: Get the lasso deep — a shallow lasso is easily stripped
Always maintain the sleeve grip — if broken, re-establish immediately
Study Braulio Estima and Michael Langhi for elite lasso guard systems
Combine lasso with spider guard on the other leg for a hybrid attack system
Use the lasso to pull them into your sweeps, not just to hold position
Practice the omoplata from lasso — it's one of the highest percentage attacks
Chain sweeps with submissions — if one fails, flow to another
Keep your free foot active — use it to push their hip or hook their leg
Don't stay flat — angle your body toward the lasso side for better attacks
Use collar grip with your free hand to add a second layer of control

guards.name: Squid Guard 🦑
guards.description: Squid Guard is a lapel-based open guard variation created by Keenan Cornelius as part of his lapel guard system. It involves threading your opponent's lapel behind their leg (similar to worm guard) but then re-gripping the lapel with your opposite hand and using it to create a cross-body tether. This configuration allows for unique sweeps, back takes, and submissions by controlling your opponent's posture and movement with the lapel wrap.
execution: Start from open guard — worm guard or De La Riva works as an entry
Pull out your opponent's far-side lapel and feed it behind their lead leg
Instead of gripping with the same-side hand (worm guard), pass the lapel to your opposite hand
The lapel now crosses your body diagonally, creating a cross-body tether
Establish a De La Riva hook on the same leg for added control
Keep constant tension on the lapel — slack neutralizes the position
Use your free hand to control their sleeve, collar, or belt
Keep your hips angled and off the mat — never lie flat
Use the cross-body tension to prevent them from stepping back or disengaging
pros: Creates powerful cross-body control that is extremely hard to pass
Opens up unique sweeps not available from standard worm guard
Excellent back take opportunities using the lapel tether
Pairs seamlessly with worm guard, ringworm, and other lapel guards
Allows you to control distance and shut down passing effectively
Difficult for opponent to strip the lapel due to the cross-body angle
Highly effective at the highest levels of Gi competition
cons: Only works in the Gi — requires lapel manipulation
Steep learning curve — requires understanding of the full lapel guard system
Takes time to set up — opponent can disrupt during the feed
Can be shut down if opponent keeps their lapel tucked tight
Requires good hip flexibility for De La Riva hook maintenance
Less common position so fewer training partners will know how to drill it
Referee may restart if the position stalls in competition
tips: Master worm guard first — squid guard builds directly on it
Study Keenan Cornelius' lapel guard encyclopedia for the complete system
Speed on the lapel transition from worm to squid is critical
Always maintain tension on the cross-body lapel grip — slack kills the position
Use the DLR hook to prevent them from stepping back to disengage
Practice transitioning between worm, squid, and ringworm fluidly
The back take is your highest-percentage attack — drill it relentlessly
If they try to untie the lapel, attack immediately while they're distracted
Combine with collar or sleeve grips for layered control
Don't rely only on the lapel — keep your legs and hips active at all times

guards.name: Deep De La Riva 🪝🕳️
guards.description: Deep De La Riva (Deep DLR) is an advanced variation of the standard De La Riva guard where your hooking leg goes much deeper — threading your entire leg through and behind the opponent's lead leg so that your foot comes out on the far side of their hip. This extra depth creates a much stronger tether, limits their mobility, and opens up powerful sweeps, back takes, and crab ride entries that are not available from the standard DLR hook.
execution: Start from standard De La Riva guard with your hook behind their lead leg
Thread your hooking leg deeper — push your foot past their far hip
Your entire shin should be behind their thigh, with your foot emerging on the far side
Control their far sleeve or collar with your top hand
Grip their ankle or pants on the hooked leg with your bottom hand
Keep your hips off the mat and angled toward the hook side
Use your free leg to frame on their far hip or bicep for distance control
Maintain constant tension — pull them forward to break their posture
Stay ready to invert, transition to crab ride, or enter berimbolo
pros: Much stronger control than standard DLR due to deeper hook
Severely limits opponent's ability to step back or disengage
Excellent gateway to crab ride and back takes
Creates powerful off-balancing for high-percentage sweeps
Pairs naturally with berimbolo and kiss of the dragon entries
Effective in both Gi and No-Gi with slight grip adjustments
Very difficult for opponent to clear the deep hook
cons: Requires excellent hip flexibility to thread the leg deeply
More difficult to enter than standard DLR
Can be vulnerable to knee slice if the hook slips to shallow DLR
Risk of knee strain if opponent applies heavy pressure on your deep hook
Requires comfort with inversions for many of the best attacks
Can be countered by backstep passes from experienced opponents
Less intuitive than standard DLR — steeper learning curve
tips: Master standard DLR first — deep DLR is a natural progression
Focus on getting the foot past their far hip — that's what makes it 'deep'
Study the Miyao Brothers and Rafa Mendes for elite deep DLR systems
Use the crab ride entry as your primary back take option
Keep constant forward pressure with the hook — never let slack develop
Practice inversions daily to build comfort going upside down
Chain deep DLR with berimbolo and kiss of the dragon for a complete system
If they start to clear the hook, transition immediately — don't fight a lost position
Use collar and sleeve grips to prevent them from establishing crossface
Combine deep DLR with standard DLR and RDLR for a layered guard game

guards.name: Sit-Up Guard 🪑
guards.description: Sit-Up Guard is an open guard position where you sit upright facing your opponent rather than lying on your back. From this seated position, you use your hands to post behind you or grip your opponent while your legs stay active in front, ready to hook, frame, or engage. It is a highly dynamic position that serves as a launching pad for arm drags, single legs, guillotines, and transitions into butterfly, DLR, or leg entanglements.
execution: Sit upright on the mat facing your opponent with your legs in front
Keep your hands posting behind you or ready to grip
Place your feet on the mat with knees bent, ready to engage
Use your feet to frame on their hips or knees to manage distance
Stay light on your seat — be ready to scoot, stand, or transition
Grip their collar, sleeve, wrist, or neck tie when in range
Keep your head up and posture strong — never slouch forward passively
Use hip scoots to maintain distance or close the gap as needed
Stay ready to insert butterfly hooks, shoot for legs, or pull guard
pros: Extremely dynamic — allows transitions to nearly any other guard
Great for initiating arm drags and single leg attacks
Works excellently in both Gi and No-Gi
Natural position for guillotine and front headlock attacks
Allows you to stand up quickly if needed
Hard for opponent to apply pressure since you're sitting up
Excellent for entering leg entanglements and butterfly guard
cons: Vulnerable to guard passes if opponent gets around your legs
Can be pushed flat if you don't maintain strong posture
Requires constant movement and energy to stay effective
Exposed to foot locks if legs are extended carelessly
Can be difficult to maintain against aggressive standing passers
Less control over opponent compared to closed or half guard
Requires good scrambling ability when exchanges happen
tips: Stay light on your butt — be ready to move in any direction
The arm drag is your best friend from sit-up guard — drill it constantly
Study Marcelo Garcia — his seated guard is the gold standard
Use your feet as your first line of defense to manage distance
Always be ready to insert butterfly hooks when they close distance
Practice the technical stand-up from seated guard for scrambles
Combine with collar drag and two-on-one grips for back takes
Never reach forward with both hands — always keep one posting behind
Hip scoot toward your opponent, don't just wait for them to come to you
Chain sit-up guard with butterfly, single leg X, and K guard entries
